title: Jane Jacobs’s urbanism without economics
gist: Yglesias delivers a harsh reappraisal of Jane Jacobs's 'The Death and Life of Great American Cities,' arguing her urbanism ignores economics and agglomeration, confuses cause with effect, and offers no method for building new great neighborhoods under modern conditions.

themes.theme: Contrarian reappraisal of a canonical text

insights.claim: Jane Jacobs's canonical 'The Death and Life of Great American Cities' holds up poorly on close reading, with large sections of Greenwich Village sociology that Yglesias calls borderline fraudulent for omitting the neighborhood's gay and bohemian character.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A sharp downgrade of a foundational urbanist text signals YIMBY thought leaders are moving past preservation-era icons toward supply economics.
insights.quote: was surprised by how much I hated it
insights.locator: And then for the podcast I actually read

insights.claim: Jacobs's selective examples undermine her credibility—she omits mafia influence when praising low street crime in Boston's North End and other Italian ethnic enclaves.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Illustrates the case that celebrated 'organic' neighborhood safety often had hidden institutional causes, not built-environment ones.
insights.locator: When she mentions the low levels of street crime

themes.theme: Cause versus effect in the built environment

insights.claim: Jacobs systematically confuses cause and effect, attributing neighborhoods' good qualities to arbitrary physical features like short blocks without establishing they are causal.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A caution against copying superficial urban-form features expecting neighborhood vibrancy; correlation is not the mechanism.
insights.quote: She also seems to have no idea how to distinguish cause from effect
insights.locator: She also seems to have no idea

insights.claim: Jacobs's confident prediction that Chelsea would never gentrify due to long blocks was proven wrong, and Barcelona's Eixample shows long blocks can coexist with great urbanism.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Physical-determinist theories of neighborhood outcomes have a poor forecasting record; discount design-only urbanist claims.
insights.locator: She expresses a lot of confidence that Chelsea

insights.claim: Jacobs's framework fails on economics: the concept of agglomeration never appears in the book, and she dismisses office-cluster districts that are actually core pillars of the urban economy.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Agglomeration, not aesthetic mix, is the real engine of urban value—the lens investors should use for city and office real estate.
insights.quote: The idea of agglomeration doesn’t appear in the book
insights.locator: We also both agree that Jacobs’s grasp of economics is weak

insights.claim: Jacobs repeats the same top-down hubris she rightly criticizes in the Garden City, Radiant City, and City Beautiful movements—appreciating classic neighborhoods but offering no method to build more of them.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: The unresolved problem is production of good urbanism at scale, which preservationist frameworks cannot answer—an opening for supply-side YIMBY policy.

insights.claim: Jacobs's framework cannot explain how modern cities like Austin or Singapore arise, or why Philadelphia keeps declining despite being widely admired by visitors.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Aesthetic appeal does not predict a city's growth or decline; economic fundamentals do—key when comparing metro real-estate trajectories.
insights.locator: You can’t understand from this book

insights.claim: Yglesias concedes Jacobs was right to be skeptical of parks and to appreciate a degree of urban crowding and happenstance, aligning with his 'Against Urban Planning' view.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Even critics retain Jacobs's density-and-serendipity intuitions; the disagreement is about economics and buildability, not density itself.
insights.locator: And I have to admit that my take
